fr:mainmatter.html:p: Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this book. As a reinforcement learning afficianado, I have a newfound fandom for the mission of Deepmind. In particular, I was at the RLC talk given by David Silver mentioned in the book. However, I must acknowledge the deep bias in favor of Demis and Deepmind in the LLM race towards the end. It is easy to come away from this, thinking that Sam Altman is some sort of Silicon Valley super villain and Demis is our champion of humanity.
If I had to give an unbiased rating, I would give this book a 6.5/10. However, as a biased reader, I give it an 8/10 :)

fr:title.text: The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind, and the Quest for Superintelligence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Infinity Machine: Demis Hassabis, DeepMind, and the Quest for Superintelligence
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{mallabyInfinityMachineDemis2026, title = {The {{Infinity Machine}}: {{Demis Hassabis}}, {{DeepMind}}, and the {{Quest}} for {{Superintelligence}}}, author = {Mallaby, Sebastian}, year = {2026}, isbn = {978-0-593-83184-7}, publisher = {Penguin Group}, keywords = {Business & Economics / Industries / Computers & Information Technology,Science / Philosophy & Social Aspects,Technology & Engineering / Social Aspects}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {E7eJEQAAQBAJ}, abstract = {An Instant New York Times BestsellerFrom one of our leading chroniclers of the intersection of innovation and capitalism, a landmark reckoning---based on unprecedented access---with one of the world's most brilliant and driven tech visionaries, and his game-changing companyEven by the standard of a tech industry stacked with so-called geniuses, Demis Hassabis is a special case. Born poor in North London to immigrant parents, a chess prodigy by age five and wizard coder in his teens, he turned down a seven figure offer before turning 18 to feed his insatiable scientific curiosity at Cambridge. Later, he added a neuroscience PhD to his computer science skills to pursue the dream of artificial general intelligence, the ultimate goal being to unravel the mysteries of biology and theoretical physics and to usher in super-abundance. Alongside a small group of fellow travelers, that is the path he is still on, leading the AI research at Google, winning a Nobel Prize along the way, and imagining machines that will compound, or possibly supplant, the human understanding of the universe.Hassabis has given Sebastian Mallaby a great deal of his time, sitting for over thirty hours of conversation. But Mallaby has also drawn from Hassabis's detractors, such as his estranged cofounder Mustafa Suleyman; from his rivals, such as OpenAI's leading scientist Ilya Sutskever; and from academic pioneers who now fear for human survival, such as Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton. The result is a revelatory account of a singular figure and his company and a profound reckoning with this protean field as it leaps from the periphery to the center of our consciousness.No one questions Hassabis's brilliance. There are those who, like Elon Musk, have at times regarded him as an "evil genius." He is in a game where the stakes are matched only by the exorbitant costs --- for talent, and for compute. Celebrated scientists pursue the technology because they cannot resist the sweetness of discovery. Others pursue it for money or power. The inventors believe they control their technology, but often, the technology controls them.Despite Hassabis's pivotal role inside Google's engine room, this is not a Silicon Valley story. Hassabis deals with the Valley and takes its money, but remains outside and furiously critical of it, lambasting its leaders in conversation with Mallaby. The end of this race cannot be known, but as this great book shows us, Hassabis's quest to will a new form of cognition into the world is a defining story for our era.}, month = {March}, shorttitle = {The {{Infinity Machine}}} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this book. As a reinforcement learning afficianado, I have a newfound fandom for the mission of Deepmind. In particular, I was at the RLC talk given by David Silver mentioned in the book. However, I must acknowledge the deep bias in favor of Demis and Deepmind in the LLM race towards the end. It is easy to come away from this, thinking that Sam Altman is some sort of Silicon Valley super villain and Demis is our champion of humanity.
If I had to give an unbiased rating, I would give this book a 6.5/10. However, as a biased reader, I give it an 8/10 :)

This was basically a historical account of the history of deep learning. The main focus was championing the likes of Hinton, Lecun, and Bengio with their unwavering conviction toward deep learning. Honestly, this book has made me excited and a bit intimidated about research. I do not know if I have the same obsessive commitment to my work as Hinton, etc. Also, for future reference, I feel like I will get a lot more out of books I am not already so familiar with. Technically, I thought it did an OK job, but I once again do not really enjoy reading books on my field for a broad audience. Learning about the industry origins i.e. DNN, Deepmind acquisition, etc. was pretty interesting though.
